---
title: "DATA3888 Group 6 - Volatility Modelling"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: false
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    theme:
      light: cosmo
      dark: darkly
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .quarto-figure,
        .quarto-float,
        .cell-output-display {
          max-width: 100%;
          overflow-x: hidden;
        }

        .quarto-figure img,
        img.figure-img {
          max-width: 100%;
          height: auto;
        }

        @media (min-width: 768px) {
          #quarto-content.toc-left.page-columns {
            column-gap: clamp(0.75rem, 2vw, 2.5rem);
            display: grid;
            grid-template-columns: minmax(150px, 170px) minmax(0, 1fr) !important;
            margin: 0;
            max-width: none;
            padding: 0 clamp(0.75rem, 1.5vw, 1.5rem);
            width: 100%;
          }

          #quarto-sidebar-toc-left {
            grid-column: 1 !important;
            width: auto !important;
          }

          #quarto-margin-sidebar {
            display: none !important;
          }

          #quarto-document-content {
            grid-column: 2 !important;
            max-width: none !important;
            width: 100% !important;
          }
        }

        #quarto-sidebar-toc-left #toc-title {
          display: none;
        }

        #quarto-sidebar-toc-left nav {
          padding-top: 0;
        }

        .quarto-color-scheme-toggle.top-right {
          display: none;
        }

        .night-mode-control {
          animation: sidebar-item-in 500ms ease both;
          margin: 0 0 1.35rem;
        }

        .night-mode-toggle {
          align-items: center;
          background: transparent;
          border: 0;
          color: var(--bs-body-color);
          display: inline-flex;
          gap: 0.65rem;
          padding: 0;
        }

        .night-mode-label {
          font-size: 0.88rem;
          letter-spacing: 0;
          opacity: 0.82;
        }

        .night-mode-switch {
          background: #101217;
          border-radius: 999px;
          box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 1px rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.08);
          display: inline-flex;
          height: 2rem;
          overflow: hidden;
          position: relative;
          transition: background-color 220ms ease, box-shadow 220ms ease, transform 220ms ease;
          width: 4rem;
        }

        .night-mode-toggle:hover .night-mode-switch,
        .night-mode-toggle:focus-visible .night-mode-switch {
          box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 1px rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.2), 0 0.35rem 1rem rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.18);
          transform: translateY(-1px);
        }

        .night-mode-toggle:focus-visible {
          outline: none;
        }

        .night-mode-toggle:focus-visible .night-mode-switch {
          box-shadow:
            inset 0 0 0 1px rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.2),
            0 0 0 0.16rem rgba(77, 171, 247, 0.32);
        }

        .night-mode-knob {
          background: #ffffff;
          border-radius: 50%;
          height: 1.55rem;
          left: 0.26rem;
          position: absolute;
          top: 0.225rem;
          transition: transform 260ms ease;
          width: 1.55rem;
          z-index: 2;
        }

        .night-mode-toggle.alternate .night-mode-knob {
          transform: translateX(1.9rem);
        }

        .night-mode-symbol {
          border-radius: 50%;
          height: 1.05rem;
          position: absolute;
          top: 0.48rem;
          transition: left 260ms ease, right 260ms ease, transform 260ms ease;
          width: 1.05rem;
          z-index: 1;
        }

        .night-mode-toggle:not(.alternate) .night-mode-symbol {
          background: #ffd338;
          right: 0.55rem;
          box-shadow:
            0 -0.48rem 0 -0.18rem #ffd338,
            0.34rem -0.34rem 0 -0.18rem #ffd338,
            0.48rem 0 0 -0.18rem #ffd338,
            0.34rem 0.34rem 0 -0.18rem #ffd338,
            0 0.48rem 0 -0.18rem #ffd338,
            -0.34rem 0.34rem 0 -0.18rem #ffd338,
            -0.48rem 0 0 -0.18rem #ffd338,
            -0.34rem -0.34rem 0 -0.18rem #ffd338;
        }

        .night-mode-toggle.alternate .night-mode-symbol {
          background: radial-gradient(circle at 68% 42%, #101217 0 42%, #ffe96a 44% 100%);
          left: 0.55rem;
          transform: rotate(-12deg);
        }

        #TOC > ul > li {
          animation: sidebar-item-in 420ms ease both;
        }

        #TOC > ul > li:nth-child(2) { animation-delay: 35ms; }
        #TOC > ul > li:nth-child(3) { animation-delay: 70ms; }
        #TOC > ul > li:nth-child(4) { animation-delay: 105ms; }
        #TOC > ul > li:nth-child(5) { animation-delay: 140ms; }
        #TOC > ul > li:nth-child(6) { animation-delay: 175ms; }
        #TOC > ul > li:nth-child(7) { animation-delay: 210ms; }
        #TOC > ul > li:nth-child(8) { animation-delay: 245ms; }
        #TOC > ul > li:nth-child(9) { animation-delay: 280ms; }

        @keyframes sidebar-item-in {
          from {
            opacity: 0;
            transform: translateX(-0.45rem);
          }
          to {
            opacity: 1;
            transform: translateX(0);
          }
        }

        @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {
          .night-mode-control,
          .night-mode-switch,
          .night-mode-knob,
          .night-mode-symbol,
          #TOC > ul > li {
            animation: none;
            transition: none;
          }
        }
        </style>
        <script>
        window.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", () => {
          window.setTimeout(() => {
            const sidebar = window.document.querySelector("#quarto-sidebar-toc-left");
            if (!sidebar || window.document.querySelector(".night-mode-control")) {
              return;
            }

            const control = window.document.createElement("div");
            control.className = "night-mode-control";
            control.innerHTML = `
              <button type="button" class="night-mode-toggle quarto-color-scheme-toggle" aria-label="Toggle night mode">
                <span class="night-mode-label">Night mode</span>
                <span class="night-mode-switch" aria-hidden="true">
                  <span class="night-mode-symbol"></span>
                  <span class="night-mode-knob"></span>
                </span>
              </button>
            `;
            sidebar.prepend(control);

            const toggle = control.querySelector(".night-mode-toggle");
            const syncToggle = () => {
              const isDark = window.document.body.classList.contains("quarto-dark");
              toggle.classList.toggle("alternate", isDark);
              toggle.setAttribute("aria-pressed", String(isDark));
            };

            toggle.addEventListener("click", () => {
              window.quartoToggleColorScheme();
              syncToggle();
            });

            syncToggle();
            new MutationObserver(syncToggle).observe(window.document.body, {
              attributes: true,
              attributeFilter: ["class"],
            });
          }, 0);
        });
        </script>
jupyter: python3
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| results: hide

from pathlib import Path
import json

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import HTML, display

from back_end.config import ARTIFACTS_DIR, FeatureConfig, INDIVIDUAL_BOOK_DIR, ROOT_DIR
from back_end.universe import build_model_comparison
from back_end.universe import build_pca_variance_explained

PROJECT_ROOT = ROOT_DIR
RAW_DATA_DIR = INDIVIDUAL_BOOK_DIR


def artifact_path(*parts: str) -> Path:
    return ARTIFACTS_DIR.joinpath(*parts)


RUNS_DIR = artifact_path("runs")
FEATURE_CACHE_PATH = artifact_path("feature_cache", "features_all_stocks.parquet")
FEATURE_CACHE_MANIFEST_PATH = artifact_path("feature_cache", "manifest.json")


def latest_run_dir() -> Path | None:
    if not RUNS_DIR.exists():
        return None
    run_dirs = sorted([path for path in RUNS_DIR.iterdir() if path.is_dir()], reverse=True)
    return run_dirs[0] if run_dirs else None


RUN_DIR = latest_run_dir()


def load_artifact(name: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    if RUN_DIR is None:
        return pd.DataFrame()
    path = RUN_DIR / name
    if not path.exists():
        return pd.DataFrame()
    return pd.read_parquet(path)


def load_feature_targets() -> pd.DataFrame:
    if not FEATURE_CACHE_PATH.exists():
        return pd.DataFrame()
    return pd.read_parquet(
        FEATURE_CACHE_PATH,
        columns=["stock_id", "time_id", "target_var", "target_vol"],
    )


def load_feature_cache() -> pd.DataFrame:
    if not FEATURE_CACHE_PATH.exists():
        return pd.DataFrame()
    return pd.read_parquet(FEATURE_CACHE_PATH)


def load_json(path: Path) -> dict:
    if not path.exists():
        return {}
    return json.loads(path.read_text())


metrics = load_artifact("metrics.parquet")
predictions = load_artifact("predictions.parquet")
feature_importance = load_artifact("feature_importance.parquet")
universe_summary = load_artifact("universe_summary.parquet")
stock_similarity = load_artifact("stock_similarity.parquet")
feature_targets = load_feature_targets()
feature_cache_manifest = load_json(FEATURE_CACHE_MANIFEST_PATH)


def model_summary_frame(metrics_df: pd.DataFrame, predictions_df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    if metrics_df.empty or predictions_df.empty:
        return pd.DataFrame()

    metric_cols = ["mse", "rmse", "mae", "mape", "rmspe", "qlike", "pearson_r"]
    available_metrics = [col for col in metric_cols if col in metrics_df.columns]
    overall = metrics_df.copy()
    if "fold" in overall.columns:
        overall = overall[overall["fold"] == 0].copy()

    summary = overall[["model", *available_metrics]].copy()
    latency = (
        predictions_df.groupby("model", as_index=False)
        .agg(
            mean_inference_us=("inference_ms", lambda values: values.mean() * 1000),
            n_predictions=("model", "size"),
        )
    )
    return summary.merge(latency, on="model", how="left")


def cached_model_comparison_table(metrics_df: pd.DataFrame, predictions_df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    columns = [
        "Model",
        "Mean inference (μs)",
        "RMSE model wins",
        "↓ QLIKE model wins",
        "RMSE",
        "QLIKE",
        "MAE",
        "MSE",
    ]
    if metrics_df.empty and predictions_df.empty:
        return pd.DataFrame(columns=columns)

    comparison = build_model_comparison(predictions_df, metrics_df)
    if comparison.empty:
        return pd.DataFrame(columns=columns)

    table = comparison[
        [
            "model",
            "mean_inference_ms",
            "best_stocks_rmse",
            "best_stocks_qlike",
            "rmse",
            "qlike",
            "mae",
            "mse",
        ]
    ].copy()
    table["mean_inference_ms"] = table["mean_inference_ms"] * 1000
    table = table.rename(
        columns={
            "model": "Model",
            "mean_inference_ms": "Mean inference (μs)",
            "best_stocks_rmse": "RMSE model wins",
            "best_stocks_qlike": "↓ QLIKE model wins",
            "rmse": "RMSE",
            "qlike": "QLIKE",
            "mae": "MAE",
            "mse": "MSE",
        }
    )
    return table.sort_values(
        ["↓ QLIKE model wins", "RMSE", "Model"],
        ascending=[False, True, True],
        na_position="last",
    ).reset_index(drop=True)


def format_report_table(frame: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    formatted = frame.copy()
    integer_cols = ["RMSE model wins", "↓ QLIKE model wins"]
    numeric_cols = [col for col in formatted.columns if col not in ["Model", *integer_cols]]
    for col in integer_cols:
        if col in formatted.columns:
            formatted[col] = formatted[col].map(lambda value: "" if pd.isna(value) else f"{int(value)}")
    for col in numeric_cols:
        formatted[col] = formatted[col].map(lambda value: "" if pd.isna(value) else f"{float(value):.3f}")
    return formatted


def pareto_optimal_mask(frame: pd.DataFrame, x_col: str, y_col: str) -> pd.Series:
    mask = []
    for _, row in frame.iterrows():
        dominated = (
            (frame[x_col] <= row[x_col])
            & (frame[y_col] <= row[y_col])
            & ((frame[x_col] < row[x_col]) | (frame[y_col] < row[y_col]))
        ).any()
        mask.append(not dominated)
    return pd.Series(mask, index=frame.index)


def stock_label(stock_id: str) -> str:
    text = str(stock_id)
    if text.startswith("stock_"):
        text = text.removeprefix("stock_")
    try:
        return f"Stock {int(text)}"
    except ValueError:
        return str(stock_id)

```

# Executive summary

Problem Description: Volatility forecasting is central to a wide range of financial applications, including option pricing, portfolio optimisation, and risk management. Competitive latency arbitrage races on exchanges have a typical duration of just 5-10 microseconds (Aquilina et al., 2021). Our project aims to help traders answer the question: "What is the best volatility prediction model for my goal, given latency constraints and given my desired model accuracy?" This is especially relevant to traders who compete in ultra high frequency domains.

Findings: Our results show a clear Pareto frontier, where a trader must choose between a faster model and a model which is more accurate. We found the XGBoost model to have the best tradeoff between inference latency and predictive accuracy, though traders competing in ultra high frequency races may choose differently.

Practical Relevance: Supporting traders in choosing volatility forecasting models under both accuracy and latency constraints. This tool helps users compare models under multiple performance metrics and computational costs, bridging statistical model performance and real-world trading requirements.

# Background

Our research question is: *what is the most accurate volatility prediction model for a given universe of stocks, given latency constraints?*

This project helps quantitative researchers and traders choose forecasting models that balance predictive accuracy with computational efficiency in latency-sensitive financial markets. The aim is to evaluate volatility models using many performance metrics to create a tool that helps users identify their desirable model based on statistical performance and real-world trading constraints.

Traders use volatility forecasting models for many purposes, including but not limited to modelling inventory risk, portfolio optimisation and value at risk (VaR) analysis (Poon and Granger, 2003). Depending on the application, traders, market makers and portfolio managers require tools which have different accuracy vs latency tradeoffs. For instance, a portfolio manager calculating their one day VaR requires the most accurate volatility model and is likely less worried about inference latency, while a researcher creating an options pricing algorithm for a highly liquid stock may prioritise speed at the cost of some accuracy.

Tools like TradingView (TradingView, n.d.) let traders view the price of a given asset over time, and introduce scripting tools which help traders visualise different functions of price, e.g., rolling volatility over time. However, they do not introduce any way for traders to predict future volatility. In order to do this, traders currently must spend significant time to code a volatility prediction model, ensure the correctness of that code, create necessary visualisations, and evaluate against different metrics. Our tool innovates on the current tooling landscape by providing a no code interface, which lets traders and market participants view model latency vs model accuracy tradeoffs in a simple, intuitive manner.

We were given two datasets to work with: a dataset of 112 anonymous, unnamed stocks, and a dataset of 10 named stocks. Each dataset contained the top two levels of orderbook data, where price series were split up into differing time_id’s. As the length of the price series within each time_id was not constant between the two datasets, we ultimately chose to model the dataset of 112 anonymous stocks, due to its higher abundance of data, and to ensure that modelling was consistent between different stocks. Notably, each time_id contains 600 seconds of data and the temporal ordering of each time_id has not been preserved.

# Methods

## Part A: Process

### Data Preprocessing

Each time_id in the dataset contains 600 seconds of order book data. When seconds were missing within a time_id, we forward filled past values to produce a continuous series without introducing future leakage. Time_ids with fewer than 20 price updates across the first 570 seconds, or fewer than 5 price updates in the final 30 seconds, were dropped to ensure models had an adequate amount of information to generate reliable predictions. The modelling target for all models was the mean realised volatility over the final 30 seconds of each time_id window.

### Feature Engineering

We computed a range of features from the observed portion of each time_id, excluding the final 30 second forecast horizon. Realised volatility features include rolling mean squared returns and volatility proxies across multiple lookback windows, along with ratios between short and long realised variance windows, following the heterogeneous autoregressive framework of Corsi (2009). Order book microstructure features capture relative bid-ask spread, order book imbalance, top-two-level depth, depth ratios, and book pressure. Volatility trend features include exponentially weighted moving averages of squared returns across four decay parameters. Return behaviour features include lag-1 and lag-5 return autocorrelation, return quantiles, zero return proportions, directional realised variance, and jump counts based on large absolute returns. Because many features are computed from overlapping return windows or related order book quantities, substantial collinearity is expected across the feature set.

To address this, our tool offers two feature modes. In Auto mode, the top N principal components of the full feature set (15 by default) are used as model inputs, extracting the maximum amount of orthogonal information while minimising multicollinearity. In Manual mode, the user selects a specific subset of features directly. The appropriate number of components to retain can be guided by the cumulative variance explained plot shown in @fig-pca-cumulative-variance, where the first 15 principal components capture approximately 82% of total feature variance, justifying the default setting.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pca-cumulative-variance
#| fig-cap: "Cumulative PCA variance."

pca_variance = build_pca_variance_explained(load_feature_cache(), FeatureConfig(), 20)

if pca_variance.empty:
    print("No cached feature data was available for the PCA cumulative variance figure.")
else:
    plot_df = pca_variance.copy()
    plot_df["cumulative_variance_ratio"] = plot_df["explained_variance_ratio"].cumsum()

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 4.8))
    ax.plot(
        plot_df["component"],
        plot_df["cumulative_variance_ratio"],
        color="#f59f00",
        linewidth=2.5,
        marker="o",
        markersize=5,
    )
    ax.set_title("Cumulative Variance Explained", fontweight="bold", loc="left")
    ax.set_xlabel("Principal component")
    ax.set_ylabel("Cumulative variance")
    ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
    ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(lambda value, _: f"{value:.0%}")
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.25)
    ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
    plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

```

### Model Training

We implemented two distinct training approaches corresponding to the two model classes. For parametric and machine learning models, including linear regression, ridge regression, LASSO, HAR-RV, decision tree, random forest, and XGBoost, we used a 5-fold cross-validation scheme stratified by stock. Each stock is assigned to one of five folds, and a single model is trained on the four remaining folds before generating out of sample predictions for every time_id in the held-out stock. This process is repeated until every stock has one prediction per model per time_id, where the model was never trained on data from that stock. This design effectively trains cross stock models that capture general volatility patterns across stocks and time_id windows, rather than stock-specific behaviour, and is consistent with large-scale forecasting comparison approaches used in the literature (Makridakis et al., 2018).

For GARCH-family models, specifically GARCH(1,1) and GJR-GARCH(1,1) (Glosten et al., 1993), the absence of temporal ordering across time_ids meant a sequential training approach was not feasible. Instead, a separate model is fitted independently on the first 570 seconds of each time_id for each stock. The fitted model is then walked forward unconditionally, producing a point variance estimate for each of the final 30 seconds, which are averaged to obtain the mean variance prediction for that window. Fits where predicted variance exceeded reasonable bounds, produced boundary parameters at or above 0.99, or generated non-finite or negative forecast paths were treated as missing to prevent unstable models from distorting aggregate results.

### Numerical Stability

Linear regression models occasionally predicted negative volatility values, causing issues in computing QLIKE loss due to the logarithmic term being unable to handle negative inputs. To address this, all negative predictions were clipped to the first percentile of training volatility for that fold. This adjustment affected only 3 out of 428,960 predictions across the dataset, but those 3 cases accounted for approximately 86% of total QLIKE loss prior to the correction, representing a distortion entirely unrelated to genuine model accuracy.

### Evaluation Framework

Model performance was assessed using four loss functions: mean squared error (MSE), root mean squared error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and QLIKE. QLIKE is the industry-standard loss metric for volatility forecasting (Patton, 2011) and serves as our primary evaluation criterion. Unlike symmetric metrics such as RMSE, QLIKE asymmetrically penalises under-predictions of volatility, which is appropriate in financial contexts where underestimating risk carries greater consequences than overestimating it (Lopez, 2001). Alongside predictive accuracy, we recorded mean inference latency in microseconds for each model, enabling direct comparison of the accuracy-latency tradeoff across the full model set. Results from prior runs are cached locally so that users do not need to re-run expensive computational workloads unnecessarily.

## Part B: Industry Problem Formulation

Volatility forecasting is central to a wide range of trading and risk management decisions, including option pricing, inventory risk management, and value at risk estimation (Poon & Granger, 2003). However, translating this financial requirement into a practical modelling system requires careful consideration of both statistical methodology and operational trading constraints, particularly inference latency. Hence, the project relies on both trading knowledge and data science methods to be used effectively.

Existing tools available to traders, such as TradingView (TradingView, n.d.), provide historical volatility visualisation and scripting capabilities but offer no mechanism for forward-looking volatility prediction or model comparison. From a data science perspective, building such a system requires decisions around feature engineering, model selection, cross validation design, and evaluation metrics. From a finance perspective, these decisions must be grounded in the realities of how traders actually use volatility forecasts, where the cost of underestimating volatility differs from overestimating it, and where inference speed can be as important as accuracy. Our choice of QLIKE as the primary evaluation metric directly reflects this financial reality, as it asymmetrically penalises under-prediction of volatility in a way that aligns with the risk management priorities of market participants (Patton, 2011).

Our tool directly addresses this gap by providing a no-code interface that allows traders and quantitative researchers to configure, run, and compare volatility models across a universe of stocks, with results presented across multiple accuracy metrics and inference latency simultaneously. @fig-workflow summarises the workflow of our product, which we cover in more detail in the Deployment Process portion of our report below.

```{python}
#| label: fig-workflow
#| fig-cap: "End-to-end volatility modelling workflow."

from matplotlib.patches import FancyArrowPatch, FancyBboxPatch, Polygon


def add_box(ax, x, y, w, h, text, color, fontsize=9.5):
    box = FancyBboxPatch(
        (x, y),
        w,
        h,
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.012,rounding_size=0.01",
        linewidth=1,
        edgecolor="#59636e",
        facecolor=color,
    )
    ax.add_patch(box)
    ax.text(x + w / 2, y + h / 2, text, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=fontsize, color="#303842")
    return (x, y, w, h)


def add_group(ax, x, y, w, h, title):
    group = FancyBboxPatch(
        (x, y),
        w,
        h,
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.01,rounding_size=0.006",
        linewidth=1,
        edgecolor="#59636e",
        facecolor="none",
    )
    ax.add_patch(group)
    ax.text(x + w / 2, y + h - 0.22, title, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=10.5, color="#303842")


def add_diamond(ax, cx, cy, w, h, text, color):
    diamond = Polygon(
        [[cx, cy + h / 2], [cx + w / 2, cy], [cx, cy - h / 2], [cx - w / 2, cy]],
        closed=True,
        linewidth=1,
        edgecolor="#59636e",
        facecolor=color,
    )
    ax.add_patch(diamond)
    ax.text(cx, cy, text, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9.0, color="#303842")
    return (cx - w / 2, cy - h / 2, w, h)


def mid_right(box):
    x, y, w, h = box
    return (x + w, y + h / 2)


def mid_left(box):
    x, y, w, h = box
    return (x, y + h / 2)


def mid_top(box):
    x, y, w, h = box
    return (x + w / 2, y + h)


def mid_bottom(box):
    x, y, w, h = box
    return (x + w / 2, y)


def add_arrow(
    ax,
    start,
    end,
    color="#59636e",
    text=None,
    text_offset=(0, 0),
    connectionstyle="arc3,rad=0",
    linestyle="-",
):
    arrow = FancyArrowPatch(
        start,
        end,
        arrowstyle="-|>",
        mutation_scale=13,
        linewidth=1.2,
        color=color,
        linestyle=linestyle,
        connectionstyle=connectionstyle,
    )
    ax.add_patch(arrow)
    if text:
        ax.text(
            (start[0] + end[0]) / 2 + text_offset[0],
            (start[1] + end[1]) / 2 + text_offset[1],
            text,
            ha="center",
            va="center",
            fontsize=8.3,
            color=color,
            fontweight="bold",
            bbox=dict(facecolor="white", edgecolor="none", pad=1.2),
        )


def add_bent_arrow(ax, points, color="#59636e", text=None, text_xy=None, linestyle="-"):
    xs, ys = zip(*points)
    ax.plot(xs[:-1], ys[:-1], color=color, linewidth=1.2, linestyle=linestyle)
    add_arrow(ax, points[-2], points[-1], color=color, linestyle=linestyle)
    if text and text_xy:
        ax.text(
            *text_xy,
            text,
            ha="center",
            va="center",
            fontsize=8.3,
            color=color,
            fontweight="bold",
            bbox=dict(facecolor="white", edgecolor="none", pad=1.2),
        )


fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(15, 8.6))
ax.set_xlim(0, 16)
ax.set_ylim(0, 8.4)
ax.axis("off")

source = "#fcebea"
process = "#ffe0a3"
model = "#cfe8ff"
decision = "#e4f1ff"
product = "#b8f0c8"

raw = add_box(ax, 0.25, 4.30, 1.55, 0.90, "Raw\norder-book\ndata", source, 9.5)
cache = add_diamond(ax, 2.90, 4.75, 1.40, 0.95, "Cached\nresults?", decision)

load = add_box(ax, 4.10, 5.70, 1.50, 0.62, "Feature\ncache", process, 9.0)
clean = add_box(ax, 4.10, 4.70, 1.50, 0.62, "Clean time\nwindows", process, 9.0)
extract = add_box(ax, 4.10, 3.70, 1.50, 0.62, "Order-book\nfeatures", process, 9.0)

features = add_box(ax, 6.70, 5.70, 1.55, 0.62, "Feature set\nor PCA", model, 9.0)
select = add_box(ax, 6.70, 4.70, 1.55, 0.62, "Selected\nmodels", model, 9.0)
model_class = add_diamond(ax, 7.48, 3.60, 1.35, 0.82, "Model\nclass", decision)
ml = add_box(ax, 9.10, 4.62, 1.42, 0.62, "ML:\n5-fold CV", model, 8.8)
garch = add_box(ax, 9.10, 3.22, 1.42, 0.62, "GARCH:\nwindow fit", model, 8.8)

forecast = add_box(ax, 11.85, 4.62, 1.55, 0.62, "Forecast final\n30 seconds", model, 8.5)
evaluate = add_box(ax, 11.85, 3.55, 1.55, 0.62, "Accuracy +\nlatency", model, 8.5)

dashboard = add_box(ax, 14.35, 4.55, 1.25, 0.82, "Dashboard\nviews", product, 9.0)
stock = add_box(ax, 14.35, 3.20, 1.25, 0.72, "Individual\nstock page", product, 8.4)
universe = add_box(ax, 14.35, 2.10, 1.25, 0.72, "Stock\nuniverse page", product, 8.4)

add_arrow(ax, mid_right(raw), mid_left(cache))
add_bent_arrow(
    ax,
    [(2.90, 4.28), (2.90, 1.20), (13.90, 1.20), (13.90, 4.96), mid_left(dashboard)],
    text="Yes",
    text_xy=(8.40, 1.00),
    linestyle="--",
)
add_bent_arrow(ax, [(3.60, 4.75), (3.78, 4.75), (3.78, 6.01), mid_left(load)], text="No", text_xy=(3.64, 5.55))
add_arrow(ax, mid_bottom(load), mid_top(clean))
add_arrow(ax, mid_bottom(clean), mid_top(extract))
add_bent_arrow(ax, [mid_right(extract), (6.15, 4.01), (6.15, 6.01), mid_left(features)])
add_arrow(ax, mid_bottom(features), mid_top(select))
add_arrow(ax, mid_bottom(select), mid_top(model_class))
add_bent_arrow(ax, [(8.15, 3.60), (8.62, 3.60), (8.62, 4.93), mid_left(ml)])
add_bent_arrow(ax, [(8.15, 3.60), (8.62, 3.60), (8.62, 3.53), mid_left(garch)])
add_bent_arrow(ax, [mid_right(ml), (11.20, 4.93), mid_left(forecast)])
add_bent_arrow(ax, [mid_right(garch), (11.20, 3.53), (11.20, 4.93), mid_left(forecast)])
add_arrow(ax, mid_bottom(forecast), mid_top(evaluate))
add_bent_arrow(ax, [mid_right(evaluate), (13.82, 3.86), (13.82, 4.96), mid_left(dashboard)])
ax.text(
    13.86,
    4.42,
    "Run\nartifacts",
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=8.3,
    color="#59636e",
    fontweight="bold",
    bbox=dict(facecolor="white", edgecolor="none", pad=1.2),
)
add_arrow(ax, mid_bottom(dashboard), mid_top(stock))
add_arrow(ax, mid_bottom(stock), mid_top(universe))

legend_y = 7.78
legend_items = [
    ("Input data", source, "box"),
    ("Processing", process, "box"),
    ("Models / metrics", model, "box"),
    ("Decision", decision, "diamond"),
    ("Dashboard output", product, "box"),
]
ax.text(0.30, legend_y, "Legend:", ha="left", va="center", fontsize=9.0, color="#303842", fontweight="bold")
legend_x = 1.20
for label, color, shape in legend_items:
    if shape == "diamond":
        add_diamond(ax, legend_x + 0.10, legend_y, 0.22, 0.18, "", color)
    else:
        add_box(ax, legend_x, legend_y - 0.09, 0.22, 0.18, "", color, 1)
    ax.text(legend_x + 0.30, legend_y, label, ha="left", va="center", fontsize=8.2, color="#303842")
    legend_x += 1.95

fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.02, right=0.98, top=0.98, bottom=0.05)
plt.show()
```

# Results

## Part A: Final Approach Justification

```{python}
#| label: tbl-model-comparison
#| tbl-cap: "Cached model comparison across all stocks. Lower error metrics are better; model wins count the number of stocks where the model had the lowest loss for that metric."

model_comparison = cached_model_comparison_table(metrics, predictions)
if model_comparison.empty:
    print("No cached model comparison results were available.")
else:
    display(format_report_table(model_comparison))

```

Using multiple forecasting loss functions and inference latency was crucial in identifying practical strengths and weaknesses of each approach. Under RMSE-based evaluation, XGBoost achieved the highest number of stock wins (31/112), followed by Random Forest (17/112) and Decision Tree (11/112). Under QLIKE, XGBoost again led with 55/112 wins, while LASSO (30/112) and GJR-GARCH(1,1) (22/112) ranked second and third, respectively. This divergence reflects the fundamentally different behaviour of the two metrics. RMSE penalises large absolute errors symmetrically, while QLIKE penalises under prediction of volatility more heavily, making it better aligned with the asymmetric risk associated with under predicting volatility.

Mean inference latency reveals significant separation between model classes. Lightweight models such as HAR-RV (0.044 μs), LASSO (0.227 μs), Ridge Regression (0.231 μs), and Decision Tree (0.295 μs) operate at sub-microsecond speeds. XGBoost (0.719 μs) remains fast despite its ensemble complexity. In contrast, GARCH-family models are substantially more expensive, with GARCH(1,1) and GJR-GARCH(1,1) being approximately 700 times slower than XGBoost. Given that latency arbitrage races typically resolve within 5-10 μs (Aquilina et al., 2021), these differences significantly impact real-time trading applications.

XGBoost has both the lowest average QLIKE loss and the most wins across stocks, with 55 wins and the lowest mean QLIKE loss of 0.496. This is followed closely by LASSO with 30 wins and mean QLIKE loss of 0.518, and trailed farther behind by GJR-GARCH(1,1) with 22 wins, though worse tail performance in QLIKE loss increases its mean QLIKE loss to 0.596. No other models win on more than 2 stocks, though we note consistent performance from the Random Forest, decision tree, and linear regression models.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pareto-frontier
#| fig-cap: "Accuracy-latency Pareto frontier."

import plotly.graph_objects as go

model_summary = model_summary_frame(metrics, predictions)
pareto_frame = model_summary.dropna(subset=["model", "mean_inference_us", "qlike"]).copy()
pareto_frame = pareto_frame[pareto_frame["mean_inference_us"] > 0].copy()

if pareto_frame.empty:
    print("No cached model metrics were available for the Pareto frontier.")
else:
    optimal = pareto_optimal_mask(pareto_frame, "mean_inference_us", "qlike")

    q1, q3 = pareto_frame["qlike"].quantile([0.25, 0.75])
    iqr = q3 - q1
    qlike_cap = max(q3 + 1.5 * iqr, pareto_frame["qlike"].median())
    qlike_cap = min(qlike_cap, pareto_frame["qlike"].max())
    pareto_frame["qlike_plot"] = pareto_frame["qlike"].clip(upper=qlike_cap)
    pareto_frame["qlike_clipped"] = pareto_frame["qlike"] > qlike_cap
    pareto_frame["plot_label"] = pareto_frame.apply(
        lambda row: f"{row['model']} ({row['qlike']:.2f})"
        if row["qlike_clipped"]
        else row["model"],
        axis=1,
    )

    pareto_models = pareto_frame[optimal].sort_values("mean_inference_us")
    dominated_models = pareto_frame[~optimal].sort_values("mean_inference_us")

    fig = go.Figure()

    if not dominated_models.empty:
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=dominated_models["mean_inference_us"],
            y=dominated_models["qlike_plot"],
            mode="markers",
            name="Dominated models",
            marker=dict(color="gray", size=10, opacity=0.7),
            text=dominated_models["model"],
            customdata=dominated_models[["qlike", "rmse", "mae", "mse"]].round(3).values,
            hovertemplate=(
                "<b>%{text}</b><br>"
                "Latency: %{x:.3f} μs<br>"
                "QLIKE: %{customdata[0]:.4f}<br>"
                "RMSE: %{customdata[1]}<br>"
                "MAE: %{customdata[2]}<br>"
                "MSE: %{customdata[3]}<br>"
                "<extra></extra>"
            ),
        ))

    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=pareto_models["mean_inference_us"],
        y=pareto_models["qlike_plot"],
        mode="lines",
        name="Pareto frontier",
        line=dict(color="red", width=2),
        hoverinfo="skip",
    ))

    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=pareto_models["mean_inference_us"],
        y=pareto_models["qlike_plot"],
        mode="markers",
        name="Pareto optimal",
        marker=dict(color="red", size=12),
        text=pareto_models["model"],
        customdata=pareto_models[["qlike", "rmse", "mae", "mse"]].round(3).values,
        hovertemplate=(
            "<b>%{text}</b><br>"
            "Latency: %{x:.3f} μs<br>"
            "QLIKE: %{customdata[0]:.4f}<br>"
            "RMSE: %{customdata[1]}<br>"
            "MAE: %{customdata[2]}<br>"
            "MSE: %{customdata[3]}<br>"
            "<extra></extra>"
        ),
    ))

    label_offsets = {
        "LASSO": (-5, 9),
        "Ridge Regression": (5, 9),
        "Decision Tree": (5, -3),
        "Linear Regression": (7, 6),
        "Random Forest": (7, -11),
        "GARCH(1,1)": (6, -10),
        "GJR-GARCH(1,1)": (6, -10),
    }
    label_alignments = {"LASSO": "right"}
    pareto_model_names = set(pareto_models["model"])

    for _, row in pareto_frame.sort_values("mean_inference_us").iterrows():
        is_optimal = row["model"] in pareto_model_names
        xshift, yshift = label_offsets.get(row["model"], (5, 5 if is_optimal else -10))
        fig.add_annotation(
            x=np.log10(row["mean_inference_us"]),
            y=row["qlike_plot"],
            text=row["plot_label"],
            showarrow=False,
            xshift=xshift,
            yshift=yshift,
            xanchor=label_alignments.get(row["model"], "left"),
            font=dict(color="red" if is_optimal else "gray", size=11),
        )

    for x_val, color, label in [
        (1, "green", "1 μs"),
        (10, "goldenrod", "10 μs"),
        (1000, "purple", "1000 μs"),
    ]:
        fig.add_vline(
            x=x_val,
            line_dash="dash",
            line_color=color,
            line_width=1.2,
            annotation_text=label,
            annotation_position="top",
            annotation_font_color=color,
        )

    fig.update_xaxes(
        type="log",
        title_text="Mean inference latency (μs, log scale)",
        showgrid=True,
        gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)",
        range=[
            np.log10(max(pareto_frame["mean_inference_us"].min() * 0.6, 0.01)),
            np.log10(pareto_frame["mean_inference_us"].max() * 2),
        ],
    )

    fig.update_yaxes(
        title_text="QLIKE (lower is better)",
        showgrid=True,
        gridcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0.1)",
        range=[
            max(pareto_frame["qlike_plot"].min() - 0.08, 0),
            pareto_frame["qlike_plot"].max() + 0.15,
        ],
    )

    fig.update_layout(
        title="Pareto Frontier: Volatility Model Selection",
        plot_bgcolor="white",
        paper_bgcolor="white",
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, xanchor="right", x=1),
        height=500,
        font=dict(family="Arial", size=12),
        margin=dict(l=60, r=40, t=80, b=60),
    )

    fig.show()
```

@fig-pareto-frontier shows the Pareto frontier of QLIKE loss against inference latency. The Pareto-optimal set consists of HAR-RV, LASSO, and XGBoost, where no alternative achieves simultaneously lower prediction error and lower latency. HAR-RV occupies the low latency extreme, XGBoost has the lowest QLIKE loss and LASSO provides an intermediate between the two. All remaining models are dominated, meaning at least one Pareto-optimal alternative performs better. Overall, we find a tradeoff between inference latency and accuracy. While XGBoost has the best overall balance between computational efficiency and predictive performance, our Pareto analysis supports that no single model is universally optimal across all evaluation criteria. Hence, model selection should depend on traders' latency tolerance and accuracy requirements.

## Part B: Deployment process

The final product is deployed as an interactive Streamlit web application, organised into three pages accessible through a fixed top navigation bar: a Model Specification page, an Individual Stock page, and a Universe page for cross-stock comparisons. To keep the tool responsive, all model results are pre-computed and cached during an initial backend run lasting between thirty minutes and four hours depending on models selected and available hardware. Once cached, results load instantly, with the specific backend run displayed to the user to ensure traceability. @fig-individual-stock-widget illustrates the Individual Stock page, where the user selects a stock and time_id to view observed realised volatility alongside forecasts from every model for the final thirty seconds. A clearly marked forecast start line separates the observed period from the forecast horizon, with held-out realised volatility overlaid for direct visual assessment.

From a data science perspective, the individual stock view lets a practitioner diagnose where models fail at a granular level, for example, whether a fast linear model like LASSO tracked a volatility spike as closely as XGBoost, or whether GARCH predictions diverged during periods of rapid order book activity. Aggregate win counts and average QLIKE scores alone obscure this stock level and window level variation. From a trader's perspective, this granularity matters because a model which performs well on average may still behave poorly on the specific assets in a trader's portfolio. By surfacing both the universe-level Pareto frontier and per stock diagnostic detail in a single interface, the dashboard gives traders the high-level model selection guidance and the asset specific insight needed to deploy a volatility model with confidence. Give the demo a try yourself below.

```{python}
#| label: fig-individual-stock-widget
#| fig-cap: "Individual stock dashboard view."

from back_end.config import DataConfig
from back_end.data import list_available_stocks, load_processed_stock
from back_end.service import build_prediction_curves


REPORT_WIDGET_STOCK_LIMIT = 12
REPORT_WIDGET_TIME_LIMIT = 20


def stock_sort_key(value: object) -> tuple[int, int, str]:
    text = str(value)
    if text.startswith("stock_"):
        suffix = text.removeprefix("stock_")
        if suffix.isdigit():
            return (0, int(suffix), text)
    return (1, 0, text)


def realised_series_from_processed(processed: pd.DataFrame, time_id: int, horizon: int) -> pd.DataFrame:
    chunk = processed[processed["time_id"] == time_id].sort_values("seconds_in_bucket").copy()
    if chunk.empty:
        return pd.DataFrame()

    returns = chunk["log_price_diff"].to_numpy(dtype=float) * 10000
    seconds = chunk["seconds_in_bucket"].to_numpy(dtype=int)
    valid = np.isfinite(returns)
    returns = returns[valid]
    seconds = seconds[valid]
    if len(returns) <= horizon:
        return pd.DataFrame()

    cutoff_idx = len(returns) - horizon
    split_second = int(seconds[cutoff_idx])
    rolling_vol = (
        pd.Series(returns ** 2)
        .rolling(30, min_periods=5)
        .mean()
        .pow(0.5)
        .to_numpy()
    )
    observed = pd.DataFrame({
        "seconds_in_bucket": seconds[:cutoff_idx],
        "realized_vol": rolling_vol[:cutoff_idx],
        "segment": "observed",
        "split_second": split_second,
        "forecast_horizon": horizon,
    })
    heldout = pd.DataFrame({
        "seconds_in_bucket": seconds[cutoff_idx:],
        "realized_vol": rolling_vol[cutoff_idx:],
        "segment": "heldout_actual",
        "split_second": split_second,
        "forecast_horizon": horizon,
    })
    return pd.concat([observed, heldout], ignore_index=True).dropna(subset=["realized_vol"])


def series_points(frame: pd.DataFrame) -> dict:
    return {
        "x": frame["seconds_in_bucket"].astype(int).tolist(),
        "y": frame["realized_vol"].round(6).tolist(),
    }


def curve_points(frame: pd.DataFrame) -> dict:
    return {
        "model": str(frame["model"].iloc[0]),
        "kind": str(frame["prediction_kind"].iloc[0]),
        "x": frame["seconds_in_bucket"].astype(int).tolist(),
        "y": frame["pred_vol"].round(6).tolist(),
    }


if predictions.empty:
    print("No cached predictions were available for the individual-stock report widget.")
else:
    run_id = RUN_DIR.name if RUN_DIR is not None else "cached run"
    raw_stocks = set(list_available_stocks(RAW_DATA_DIR))
    available_stocks = sorted(
        set(predictions["stock_id"].dropna().unique().tolist()).intersection(raw_stocks),
        key=stock_sort_key,
    )
    available_time_ids = [int(value) for value in sorted(predictions["time_id"].dropna().unique())]
    if not raw_stocks:
        print(f"No raw stock CSV subset was found in {RAW_DATA_DIR}. The report still renders from cached artifacts, but the individual-stock widget is omitted.")
    elif not available_stocks:
        print("The raw stock CSV subset does not overlap the cached prediction stock IDs, so the individual-stock widget is omitted.")
    else:
        widget_stocks = available_stocks[:REPORT_WIDGET_STOCK_LIMIT]
        widget_time_ids = available_time_ids[:REPORT_WIDGET_TIME_LIMIT]
        if "stock_0" in available_stocks and "stock_0" not in widget_stocks:
            widget_stocks = ["stock_0", *widget_stocks[:-1]]
        if 5 in available_time_ids and 5 not in widget_time_ids:
            widget_time_ids = [5, *widget_time_ids[:-1]]
        widget_time_ids = sorted(set(widget_time_ids))

        horizon = int(load_json(RUN_DIR / "config.json").get("features", {}).get("forecast_horizon", 30)) if RUN_DIR else 30
        data_payload = {}
        data_config = DataConfig(source_dir=str(RAW_DATA_DIR))

        for stock_id in widget_stocks:
            processed = load_processed_stock(stock_id, data_config)
            stock_payload = {}
            stock_predictions = predictions[predictions["stock_id"] == stock_id]
            for time_id in widget_time_ids:
                realised = realised_series_from_processed(processed, time_id, horizon)
                if realised.empty:
                    continue
                selected_predictions = stock_predictions[stock_predictions["time_id"] == time_id].reset_index(drop=True)
                curves = build_prediction_curves(realised, selected_predictions)
                observed = realised[realised["segment"] == "observed"]
                heldout = realised[realised["segment"] == "heldout_actual"]
                forecast_curves = []
                if not curves.empty and "model" in curves.columns:
                    forecast_curves = [
                        curve_points(frame)
                        for _, frame in curves.groupby("model", sort=False)
                        if not frame.empty
                    ]
                stock_payload[str(time_id)] = {
                    "observed": series_points(observed),
                    "heldout": series_points(heldout),
                    "splitSecond": int(realised["split_second"].dropna().iloc[0]),
                    "maxSecond": int(realised["seconds_in_bucket"].max()),
                    "curves": forecast_curves,
                }
            if stock_payload:
                data_payload[stock_id] = stock_payload

        widget_data = {
            "runId": run_id,
            "defaultStock": "stock_0" if "stock_0" in data_payload else next(iter(data_payload), ""),
            "defaultTimeId": "5",
            "stocks": list(data_payload),
            "series": data_payload,
        }
        widget_json = json.dumps(widget_data).replace("</", "<\\/")

        display(HTML(f"""
    <div id="individual-stock-report-widget" class="individual-stock-widget">
      <style>
        .individual-stock-widget {{
          background: #0e1117;
          color: #f8fafc;
          border-radius: 8px;
          padding: 1.2rem 1.35rem 1.4rem;
          margin: 1rem 0;
          font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
        }}
        .individual-stock-widget h3 {{
          margin: 0 0 1rem;
          font-size: 1.45rem;
          line-height: 1.2;
        }}
        .individual-stock-controls {{
          display: grid;
          grid-template-columns: minmax(0, 3fr) minmax(160px, 1fr);
          gap: 1rem;
          margin-bottom: 0.75rem;
        }}
        .individual-stock-control label {{
          display: block;
          color: #d7dce2;
          font-size: 0.78rem;
          font-weight: 650;
          margin-bottom: 0.45rem;
        }}
        .individual-stock-control select {{
          width: 100%;
          background: #262933;
          color: #f8fafc;
          border: 1px solid #262933;
          border-radius: 7px;
          padding: 0.62rem 0.75rem;
          font-size: 0.9rem;
        }}
        .individual-stock-caption {{
          color: #aab1bd;
          font-size: 0.78rem;
          margin: 0.4rem 0 1.2rem;
        }}
        .individual-stock-caption code {{
          color: #35b779;
          background: transparent;
          padding: 0;
        }}
        .individual-stock-chart-title {{
          font-weight: 700;
          color: #f8fafc;
          margin: 0 0 0.45rem;
        }}
        .individual-stock-chart-message {{
          align-items: center;
          color: #aab1bd;
          display: flex;
          height: 100%;
          justify-content: center;
          text-align: center;
        }}
        .individual-stock-note {{
          color: #aab1bd;
          font-size: 0.75rem;
          margin-top: 0.55rem;
        }}
        @media (max-width: 760px) {{
          .individual-stock-controls {{
            grid-template-columns: 1fr;
          }}
        }}
      </style>
      <h3>Individual Stock</h3>
      <div class="individual-stock-controls">
        <div class="individual-stock-control">
          <label for="report-stock-select">Select stock</label>
          <select id="report-stock-select"></select>
        </div>
        <div class="individual-stock-control">
          <label for="report-time-select">Time window (10-min period)</label>
          <select id="report-time-select"></select>
        </div>
      </div>
      <div class="individual-stock-caption">Showing backend run <code>{run_id}</code>.</div>
      <div id="report-chart-title" class="individual-stock-chart-title"></div>
      <div id="report-individual-stock-chart" style="width:100%;height:520px;"></div>
      <div class="individual-stock-note">
        Report widget embeds {len(widget_stocks)} stocks and {len(widget_time_ids)} time windows to keep the HTML responsive; the Streamlit app supports the full cached run.
      </div>
      <script src="https://cdn.plot.ly/plotly-2.35.2.min.js"></script>
      <script>
      (() => {{
        const data = {widget_json};
        const colors = ["#4dabf7", "#dee2e6", "#90be6d", "#ffd43b", "#ffb703", "#f15bb5", "#00bbf9", "#ffe066", "#8ecae6", "#fb8500", "#9b5de5"];
        const widget = document.getElementById("individual-stock-report-widget");
        const stockSelect = widget.querySelector("#report-stock-select");
        const timeSelect = widget.querySelector("#report-time-select");
        const chartTitle = widget.querySelector("#report-chart-title");
        const chart = widget.querySelector("#report-individual-stock-chart");

        function stockOrder(value) {{
          const match = String(value).match(/^stock_(\\d+)$/);
          return match ? Number(match[1]) : Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER;
        }}

        function populateStocks() {{
          data.stocks.sort((a, b) => stockOrder(a) - stockOrder(b)).forEach((stock) => {{
            const option = document.createElement("option");
            option.value = stock;
            option.textContent = stock;
            stockSelect.appendChild(option);
          }});
          if (data.defaultStock) stockSelect.value = data.defaultStock;
        }}

        function populateTimes() {{
          const stockPayload = data.series[stockSelect.value] || {{}};
          const selected = timeSelect.value;
          timeSelect.innerHTML = "";
          Object.keys(stockPayload).map(Number).sort((a, b) => a - b).forEach((timeId) => {{
            const option = document.createElement("option");
            option.value = String(timeId);
            option.textContent = String(timeId);
            timeSelect.appendChild(option);
          }});
          if (stockPayload[selected]) {{
            timeSelect.value = selected;
          }} else if (stockPayload[data.defaultTimeId]) {{
            timeSelect.value = data.defaultTimeId;
          }}
        }}

        function lineTrace(points, name, color, dash = "solid", width = 2.4) {{
          return {{
            x: points.x,
            y: points.y,
            type: "scatter",
            mode: "lines",
            name,
            line: {{ color, width, dash }},
            hovertemplate: "Time: %{{x}}s<br>Volatility: %{{y:.6f}}<extra>" + name + "</extra>"
          }};
        }}

        function render() {{
          const stock = stockSelect.value;
          const timeId = timeSelect.value;
          const payload = data.series?.[stock]?.[timeId];
          chartTitle.textContent = `Realised Volatility — ${{stock}} · Window ${{timeId}}`;

          if (!payload) {{
            chart.innerHTML = '<div class="individual-stock-chart-message">No cached data is available for this stock and time window.</div>';
            return;
          }}
          if (!window.Plotly) {{
            chart.innerHTML = '<div class="individual-stock-chart-message">The chart library did not load. Reopen the report with an internet connection and refresh.</div>';
            return;
          }}

          const traces = [
            lineTrace(payload.observed, "Observed realised volatility", colors[0], "solid", 2.6),
            lineTrace(payload.heldout, "Held-out realised volatility", colors[1], "dot", 2.4),
          ];
          payload.curves.forEach((curve, index) => {{
            traces.push(lineTrace(
              {{ x: curve.x, y: curve.y }},
              `${{curve.model}} forecast`,
              colors[(index + 2) % colors.length],
              curve.kind === "garch_path" ? "solid" : "dash",
              2.1
            ));
          }});

          const maxY = Math.max(
            0.001,
            ...traces.flatMap((trace) => trace.y || []).filter((value) => Number.isFinite(value))
          );
          const layout = {{
            height: 520,
            margin: {{ t: 82, r: 14, b: 52, l: 64 }},
            paper_bgcolor: "#0e1117",
            plot_bgcolor: "#141922",
            font: {{ color: "#dce2ea", family: "Arial, sans-serif", size: 12 }},
            xaxis: {{
              title: {{ text: "Time (seconds)" }},
              range: [0, 600],
              tickvals: [0, 60, 120, 180, 240, 300, 360, 420, 480, 540, 600],
              ticktext: ["0m", "1m", "2m", "3m", "4m", "5m", "6m", "7m", "8m", "9m", "10m"],
              gridcolor: "rgba(210, 220, 230, 0.16)",
              zerolinecolor: "rgba(210, 220, 230, 0.18)"
            }},
            yaxis: {{
              title: {{ text: "Realised Volatility" }},
              range: [0, maxY * 1.08],
              tickformat: ".4f",
              gridcolor: "rgba(210, 220, 230, 0.16)",
              zerolinecolor: "rgba(210, 220, 230, 0.18)"
            }},
            legend: {{
              orientation: "h",
              yanchor: "bottom",
              y: 1.08,
              xanchor: "left",
              x: 0,
              font: {{ size: 11 }}
            }},
            hovermode: "x unified",
            shapes: [
              {{
                type: "rect",
                xref: "x",
                yref: "paper",
                x0: 0,
                x1: payload.splitSecond,
                y0: 0,
                y1: 1,
                fillcolor: "rgba(77, 171, 247, 0.05)",
                line: {{ width: 0 }},
                layer: "below"
              }},
              {{
                type: "rect",
                xref: "x",
                yref: "paper",
                x0: payload.splitSecond,
                x1: payload.maxSecond,
                y0: 0,
                y1: 1,
                fillcolor: "rgba(251, 133, 0, 0.08)",
                line: {{ width: 0 }},
                layer: "below"
              }},
              {{
                type: "line",
                xref: "x",
                yref: "paper",
                x0: payload.splitSecond,
                x1: payload.splitSecond,
                y0: 0,
                y1: 1,
                line: {{ color: "#adb5bd", width: 1.5, dash: "dot" }}
              }}
            ],
            annotations: [{{
              x: payload.splitSecond,
              y: 1,
              xref: "x",
              yref: "paper",
              text: "Forecast start",
              showarrow: false,
              xanchor: "left",
              yanchor: "bottom",
              font: {{ color: "#adb5bd", size: 11 }}
            }}]
          }};

          Plotly.react(chart, traces, layout, {{ responsive: true, displayModeBar: false }});
        }}

        function renderWhenPlotlyIsReady() {{
          if (window.Plotly) {{
            render();
          }} else {{
            window.setTimeout(render, 1200);
          }}
        }}

        populateStocks();
        populateTimes();
        stockSelect.addEventListener("change", () => {{
          populateTimes();
          renderWhenPlotlyIsReady();
        }});
        timeSelect.addEventListener("change", renderWhenPlotlyIsReady);
        renderWhenPlotlyIsReady();
      }})();
      </script>
    </div>
    """))

```

# Discussion

Our results show that no single volatility model is universally optimal across all evaluation criteria. Model selection should instead depend on the trader's objective function, latency requirements and asset universe. Hence, we should evaluate models using multiple measures instead of a single accuracy metric. We found that XGBoost produces the strongest overall performance across both the RMSE and QLIKE loss functions while maintaining low inference latency. We reiterate that this tradeoff between inference latency and volatility prediction accuracy shown in @fig-pareto-frontier is the core finding of our analysis.

From a data science perspective, our analysis was performed using a static historical dataset containing 112 anonymised stocks. While justifiable in controlled experimentation, the dynamic nature of financial markets is not fully captured. Market regimes, volatility clustering patterns and liquidity conditions evolve over time, so model performance may differ when deployed on live market data. Future work may extend our volatility models to incorporate the most up to date market data and continuously re-fit on live market data to generate the most accurate predictions.

From a finance perspective, traders are primarily concerned with the economic value associated with forecasts, including profitability, transaction costs, market impact and risk adjusted returns. A model which produces marginally better volatility forecasts may not produce superior trading performance once these practical considerations are incorporated. Furthermore, since the stocks are anonymised, it is not possible to investigate if model performance varies systematically across sectors, market capitalisations or liquidity profiles. Future work may add more systematic features and incorporate information from additional data feeds such as trades or news.

Currently, users are limited to selecting from a predefined set of implemented models. To increase flexibility, users should be able to import their own custom forecasting models and compare them alongside existing approaches. From a modelling perspective, models are traditionally trained using standard objectives (e.g., RSS) when they are later evaluated using volatility specific metrics (e.g., QLIKE). Future work may investigate models trained directly to minimize user specified objective functions, allowing the optimisation of models for metrics most relevant to their application. Additionally, since our analysis was limited to a predefined set of volatility models and hyperparameter configurations, more advanced machine learning architectures and automated hyperparameter optimisation may further improve predictive performance, particularly if the computational power available to a user is increased.

# Conclusion

In conclusion, we built a tool that helps traders choose the best model for their specific use case, by simultaneously evaluating model accuracy against latency for any combination of assets and loss functions. While the current implementation is limited by static data and a pre-defined list of models, our tool provides a strong foundation for future work. In particular, we suggest future authors expand the product to let traders bring in their own models and integrate the tool with additional or live data feeds. Our product helps outline the clear Pareto frontier that exists between volatility prediction model latency and accuracy, informing traders which model best fits their use case.

# Student contributions

Eriks Reinfelds wrote most of the front end, back end and caching layer (to ensure heavy results did not need to be unnecessarily recomputed) of the product. He also wrote part of the draft for the final report.

Zhan Su contributed to the front end (universe page), the first and second versions of the report code, and figure/table generation.

Zhan participated in writing the Results and Conclusion sections of the report. Wendi Ding contributed to backend model exploration, model evaluation, testing, and part of the report.

Vania Kumar performed the backend model development by completing data exploration and implementing baseline volatility models. She also prepared and formatted the project presentation and drafted initial sections of the report.

Kin Wang Yau contributed to the backend code, particularly the implementation of the models.py file which correctly specifies each of the models. Kin helped ensure the team was prepared for our presentation.

Ayush Arora was involved in the ideation phase, contributing to discussions around clustering and individual stock comparisons. He assisted in the implementation of the back end. Ayush designed the presentation template and helped write and polish the final report.

# AI Acknowledgement

Generative AI was used to assist in planning the structure of this report, editing, proof reading and programming. We note that a first draft of the report was entirely hand written and proofed by humans before being passed over with AI for brevity and to improve the precision of how we worded our ideas. Model used: GPT 5.5 thinking.

# References

Aquilina, M., Budish, E., & O'Neill, P. (2021). Quantifying the high frequency trading "arms race" (BIS Working Paper No. 955). Bank for International Settlements. https://www.bis.org/publ/work955.pdf

Corsi, F. (2009). A simple approximate long-memory model of realized volatility. Journal of Financial Econometrics, 7(2), 174–196. https://doi.org/10.1093/jjfinec/nbp001

Glosten, L. R., Jagannathan, R., & Runkle, D. E. (1993). On the relation between the expected value and the volatility of the nominal excess return on stocks. Journal of Finance, 48(5), 1779–1801. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1540-6261.1993.tb05128.x

Lopez, J. A. (2001). Evaluating the predictive accuracy of volatility models. Journal of Forecasting, 20(2), 87–109. https://doi.org/10.1002/1099-131X(200103)20:2\<87::AID-FOR782\>3.0.CO;2-7

Makridakis, S., Spiliotis, E., & Assimakopoulos, V. (2018). The M4 competition: Results, findings, conclusion and way forward. International Journal of Forecasting, 34(4), 802–808. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijforecast.2018.06.001

Patton, A. J. (2011). Volatility forecast comparison using imperfect volatility proxies. Journal of Econometrics, 160(1), 246–256. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeconom.2010.03.034

Poon, S. H., & Granger, C. W. J. (2003). Forecasting volatility in financial markets: A review. Journal of Economic Literature, 41(2), 478–539. https://doi.org/10.1257/002205103765762743

TradingView. (n.d.). Visuals overview. Pine Script documentation. https://www.tradingview.com/pine-script-docs/visuals/overview/


# Appendix

The appendix records supporting definitions, run metadata, and additional static outputs from the volatility modelling product. These materials are excluded from the main report because they support reproducibility and interpretation, rather than changing the core conclusion that model selection depends on the user's accuracy-latency tradeoff.

## A.1 Terminology

| Term | Description |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Realised volatility | The observed volatility computed from high-frequency log returns within a time window. In this project it is the target the models attempt to forecast. |
| Forecast horizon | The final 30 seconds of each 600-second time_id window. Features are computed before this horizon, while model predictions are evaluated against this held-out interval. |
| QLIKE | The main volatility-forecast loss used in the report. It is useful for risk applications because under-predicting volatility is penalised more heavily than over-predicting it. |
| Inference latency | The recorded time required to generate a prediction after features are available. This is reported in microseconds to connect model performance with latency-sensitive trading use cases. |
| PCA feature mode | A feature mode where correlated raw features are transformed into principal components before modelling. The cached production run used 15 principal components for the fitted models. |
| Manual feature mode | A product mode where the user selects specific engineered features directly instead of using PCA components. |
| Pareto frontier | The set of models where no other model is both faster and more accurate under the selected metric. |
| Cross-stock validation | The validation design where each fold holds out entire stocks, so predictions for a stock come from a model not trained on that stock. |
| GARCH window fit | The GARCH-family approach used here, where a separate model is fitted within each time_id window because the original chronological ordering of time_id values is unavailable. |
| Cached run artifact | A stored output from the backend pipeline, such as metrics, predictions, feature importance, or stock similarity. The dashboard reads these files instead of recomputing models interactively. |

## A.2 Cached Run Metadata

```{python}
#| label: tbl-appendix-run-metadata
#| tbl-cap: "Metadata for the cached backend run used by the report."

run_config = load_json(RUN_DIR / "config.json") if RUN_DIR is not None else {}
run_status = load_json(RUN_DIR / "status.json") if RUN_DIR is not None else {}
feature_config = run_config.get("features", {})
data_config = run_config.get("data", {})

metadata_rows = [
    ("Run ID", RUN_DIR.name if RUN_DIR is not None else "No cached run"),
    ("Pipeline version", run_status.get("pipeline_version", feature_cache_manifest.get("pipeline_version", ""))),
    ("Run status", run_status.get("status", "")),
    ("Stocks modelled", run_status.get("n_stocks", len(data_config.get("stocks", [])))),
    ("Feature rows", f"{int(run_status.get('n_feature_rows', 0)):,}" if run_status else ""),
    ("Predictions", f"{int(run_status.get('n_predictions', 0)):,}" if run_status else ""),
    ("Cross-validation folds", run_status.get("n_folds", "")),
    ("Forecast horizon", f"{feature_config.get('forecast_horizon', '')} seconds"),
    ("Feature mode", feature_config.get("feature_mode", "")),
    ("PCA components in run config", feature_config.get("n_pca_components", "")),
    ("Feature source", run_status.get("feature_source", "")),
    ("Feature cache created", feature_cache_manifest.get("created_at_utc", "")),
]

display(pd.DataFrame(metadata_rows, columns=["Item", "Value"]))
```

## A.3 Static Product Outputs

The figures below are static versions of product-level outputs. They are generated from the same cached artifacts used by the Streamlit dashboard, but they avoid embedding another live interface inside the report.

```{python}
#| label: fig-appendix-model-wins
#| fig-cap: "Number of stocks won by each model under RMSE and QLIKE."

appendix_comparison = build_model_comparison(predictions, metrics)
if appendix_comparison.empty:
    print("No cached model comparison data was available.")
else:
    wins = appendix_comparison[
        ["model", "best_stocks_rmse", "best_stocks_qlike"]
    ].copy()
    wins = wins.sort_values("best_stocks_qlike", ascending=True)

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.5, 5.6))
    y = np.arange(len(wins))
    width = 0.38
    ax.barh(y - width / 2, wins["best_stocks_rmse"], height=width, label="RMSE wins", color="#4dabf7")
    ax.barh(y + width / 2, wins["best_stocks_qlike"], height=width, label="QLIKE wins", color="#f59f00")
    ax.set_yticks(y)
    ax.set_yticklabels(wins["model"])
    ax.set_xlabel("Number of stocks")
    ax.set_title("Per-stock model wins", fontweight="bold", loc="left")
    ax.grid(axis="x", alpha=0.25)
    ax.legend(frameon=False, loc="lower right")
    ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-appendix-feature-importance
#| fig-cap: "Largest average PCA-component importances across fitted models."

if feature_importance.empty:
    print("No cached feature importance data was available.")
else:
    importance = (
        feature_importance.groupby(["model", "feature"], as_index=False)["importance"]
        .mean()
        .sort_values("importance", ascending=False)
    )
    top_features = importance.groupby("feature", as_index=False)["importance"].sum()
    top_features = top_features.nlargest(10, "importance")["feature"].tolist()
    plot_importance = importance[importance["feature"].isin(top_features)].copy()
    plot_importance = plot_importance.pivot_table(
        index="feature",
        columns="model",
        values="importance",
        aggfunc="mean",
        fill_value=0,
    )
    plot_importance = plot_importance.loc[top_features]

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5.8))
    plot_importance.plot(kind="bar", ax=ax, width=0.82)
    ax.set_title("Feature importance by principal component", fontweight="bold", loc="left")
    ax.set_xlabel("Principal component")
    ax.set_ylabel("Average importance")
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.25)
    ax.legend(title="Model", frameon=False, ncol=2, fontsize=8)
    ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
    plt.xticks(rotation=0)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-appendix-individual-static
#| fig-cap: "Static individual-stock forecast example from the cached product run."

from back_end.config import DataConfig
from back_end.data import list_available_stocks, load_processed_stock
from back_end.service import build_prediction_curves


if predictions.empty:
    print("No cached predictions were available for the static individual-stock example.")
else:
    raw_stocks = set(list_available_stocks(RAW_DATA_DIR))
    prediction_stocks = set(predictions["stock_id"].dropna().unique())
    available_stocks = sorted(raw_stocks.intersection(prediction_stocks), key=stock_sort_key)

    if not raw_stocks:
        print(f"No raw stock CSV subset was found in {RAW_DATA_DIR}. The static individual-stock example is omitted.")
    elif not available_stocks:
        print("The raw stock CSV subset does not overlap the cached prediction stock IDs, so the static individual-stock example is omitted.")
    else:
        example_stock = "stock_10" if "stock_10" in available_stocks else available_stocks[0]
        available_times = sorted(predictions.loc[predictions["stock_id"] == example_stock, "time_id"].dropna().unique())
        example_time = 5 if 5 in available_times else int(available_times[0])
        horizon = int(feature_config.get("forecast_horizon", 30))

        processed = load_processed_stock(example_stock, DataConfig(source_dir=str(RAW_DATA_DIR)))
        realised = realised_series_from_processed(processed, example_time, horizon)
        selected_predictions = predictions[
            (predictions["stock_id"] == example_stock)
            & (predictions["time_id"] == example_time)
        ].reset_index(drop=True)
        selected_predictions = selected_predictions[
            selected_predictions["model"].isin(["HAR-RV", "LASSO", "XGBoost", "GJR-GARCH(1,1)"])
        ].copy()
        curves = build_prediction_curves(realised, selected_predictions)

        if realised.empty or curves.empty:
            print("No realised series or forecast curves were available for the selected stock/time_id.")
        else:
            fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5.8))
            observed = realised[realised["segment"] == "observed"]
            heldout = realised[realised["segment"] == "heldout_actual"]
            split_second = int(realised["split_second"].dropna().iloc[0])

            ax.plot(
                observed["seconds_in_bucket"],
                observed["realized_vol"],
                color="#4dabf7",
                linewidth=2.2,
                label="Observed realised volatility",
            )
            ax.plot(
                heldout["seconds_in_bucket"],
                heldout["realized_vol"],
                color="#495057",
                linewidth=2.0,
                linestyle=":",
                label="Held-out realised volatility",
            )

            palette = {
                "HAR-RV": "#2f9e44",
                "LASSO": "#845ef7",
                "XGBoost": "#f59f00",
                "GJR-GARCH(1,1)": "#e03131",
            }
            for model, frame in curves.groupby("model", sort=False):
                frame = frame.sort_values("seconds_in_bucket")
                ax.plot(
                    frame["seconds_in_bucket"],
                    frame["pred_vol"],
                    linewidth=1.8,
                    linestyle="--" if frame["prediction_kind"].iloc[0] != "garch_path" else "-",
                    color=palette.get(model, "#6c757d"),
                    label=f"{model} forecast",
                )

            ax.axvline(split_second, color="#868e96", linewidth=1.4, linestyle="--")
            ax.text(split_second + 3, ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.96, "Forecast start", color="#868e96", fontsize=9, va="top")
            ax.set_title(f"Individual stock view: {stock_label(example_stock)} / time_id {example_time}", fontweight="bold", loc="left")
            ax.set_xlabel("Seconds in bucket")
            ax.set_ylabel("Realised volatility")
            ax.grid(alpha=0.22)
            ax.legend(frameon=False, ncol=2, fontsize=8)
            ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
            ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
            plt.tight_layout()
            plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-appendix-stock-similarity
#| fig-cap: "Realised-volatility similarity heatmap for a high-volatility subset of stocks."

if stock_similarity.empty or universe_summary.empty:
    print("No cached stock similarity data was available.")
else:
    top_stocks = (
        universe_summary.sort_values("mean_volatility", ascending=False)
        .head(18)["stock_id"]
        .tolist()
    )
    similarity_subset = stock_similarity.loc[top_stocks, top_stocks].copy()
    labels = [stock_label(stock) for stock in top_stocks]

    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9.5, 8.0))
    image = ax.imshow(similarity_subset.to_numpy(dtype=float), cmap="RdBu_r", vmin=-1, vmax=1, aspect="auto")
    ax.set_xticks(np.arange(len(labels)))
    ax.set_yticks(np.arange(len(labels)))
    ax.set_xticklabels(labels, rotation=45, ha="right", fontsize=8)
    ax.set_yticklabels(labels, fontsize=8)
    ax.set_title("Stock similarity by target variance correlation", fontweight="bold", loc="left")
    cbar = fig.colorbar(image, ax=ax, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)
    cbar.set_label("Correlation")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
```
